content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 6, 1988

Melvin G. Cooper, Executive Director
State Ethics Commission
817 South Court Street, Suite 2B Annex
Montgomery, AL 36104

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-88-314

Dear Mr. Cooper:

We have received your August 1, 1988 letter regarding
California's Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTION

Does the Political Reform Act prohibit state university and
college faculty from selling complimentary textbooks provided
by textbook publishers?

CONCLUSION

The Political Reform Act does not prohibit state university
and college faculty from selling books. The income received,
however, may need to be disclosed by faculty members.

DISCUSSION

The Political Reform Act (enclosed) was a ballot initiative
approved overwhelmingly by the voters in 1974. The Act was
designed to accomplish several purposes. It requires the
public disclosure of campaign contributions and expenditures
during elections, regulates the activities of lobbyists, and
requires public officials to disclose their private financial
interests and, in appropriate circumstances, disqualify
themselves from participating in decisions which may affect
those interests.

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code
of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code
of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Melvin G. Cooper
September 6, 1988
Page 2


     The Act requires public officials who participate in
governmental decisionmaking to disclose their financial
interests that could be affected in the course of their
official duties.  Financial interests which must be disclosed
are investments, real property interests, and sources of income
(including loans and gifts).  (Sections 87200-87313.)

     State college and university faculty members generally do
not participate in governmental decisions which affect their
private financial interests.  The Commission has considered the
potential for conflicts of interest in academic decisions and
has adopted a regulation on the subject.  Regulation 18705
(copy enclosed) exempts teaching decisions, including selection
of textbooks, from the conflict-of-interest laws.

     The Act does not prohibit faculty members from selling
complimentary textbooks provided by textbook publishers.
Receipt of a textbook is not a "gift" to the faculty members
for purposes of the Act.  (Section 82028(b)(1).)  Thus, no
faculty member would be required to disclose receipt of
textbooks from publishers.  However, if any faculty member is
required to disclose his or her financial interests, he or she
would be required to report income totaling $250 or more in a
year that he or she received from any person as a result of
textbook sales.  (Sections 87207(a) and 87302(b).)

     I hope this answers your questions satisfactorily.  If we
can be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact
us at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

                                        [illegible]
                                   By: Jeanette E. Turvill
                                        Political Reform Consultant
                                        Legal Division

Enclosures

STATE OF ALABAMA
STATE ETHICS COMMISSION
817 SOUTH COURT STREET
SUITE 2B ANNEX
MONTGOMERY, ALABAMA 36104

August 1, 1988

TELEPHONE (205) 261-2997

Melvin G. Cooper
Executive Director

Howard E McKenzie
Assistant Director

James J. Hicks, M.D
Cordell Wynn, Ph.D
Sandra X. Martin, Ph.D
James W. Anderson, Esq
J. Ray Warren


Mr. John Keplinger
Director
California Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street, Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Keplinger:

     I have attached a copy of Advisory Opinion No. 1155 approved
by the members of the Alabama Ethics Commission on November 13,
1987.

     Since this Opinion was issued, considerable controversy has
arisen from groups and textbook publishers supporting the Opinion
and groups of faculty members and small textbook publishers vigor-
ously opposing the Opinion. As a result, the Ethics Commission
members directed me to canvass other States having Ethics laws and
ask the following questions:

     1.  Has your Commission or Board considered a similar
         question and, if so, how did they rule? (Would you be
         kind enough to send us a copy of the Advisory Opinion.)

     2.  If not, what is your personal opinion based on the
         wording of your law as to how your Commission or Board
         would rule if a similar situation confronted them?

     We always appreciate your help, and I hope that you can give
me a quick answer since the Alabama Ethics Commission meets in
three weeks.

     With continued warm personal regards to you, I remain,

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Melvin G. Cooper
                                        Executive Director

MGC/bh

Atch

ADVISORY OPINION NO. 1155

FACTS:

In a promotional effort to encourage professors in in-
stitutions of higher learning to use their textbooks, pub-
lishers furnish faculty members with complimentary copies
of their publications. Those textbooks not selected for use
in their classes by professors are often sold to bookstores
and/or individuals for personal profit.

QUESTION:

May faculty members at institutions of higher learning
sell complimentary copies of textbooks furnished by textbook
publishers without violating the Alabama Ethics law?

OPINION:

NO. While the Alabama Ethics Commission recognizes that
the only method faculty members have to select the correct
textbook for a particular course of instruction is to review
the various books in that field, it is also recognized that
complimentary copies of texts are furnished faculty members
because of their public positions in the hope that such texts
will be chosen for use. Consequently, it is the opinion
of the Commission that those texts which faculty members do
not keep for their personal use be donated to the institu-
tional library so as to avoid any possible violation of
Section 36-25-5(a), Code of Alabama 1975 (the Ethics law),
which reads as follows:

"No public official or employee shall use an offi-
cial position or office to obtain direct personal
financial gain for himself, or his family, or any
business with which he or a member of his family
is associated unless such use and gain are specif-
ically authorized by law."

The foregoing is in keeping with Section 16-36-5, Code
of Alabama 1975, which provides that samples of textbooks
provided by publishers to local textbook committees which
are not adopted and not reclaimed by the publishers shall
become the property of the library service.

AUTHORITY:

Vote of the Commission on November 13, 1987.

[illegible]
James J. Hicks
Chairman
Alabama Ethics Commission

EB -314

817 SOUTH COURT STREET

MONTGOMERY, ALABAMA 36104

     Since this Opinion was issued, considerable controversy has
arisen from groups and textbook publishers supporting the Opinion

     1. Has your Commission or Board considered a similar

     2. If not, what is your personal opinion based on the

     We always appreciate your help, and I hope that you can give

                                   Sincerely,

                                   [signature]
